skill.name: flutter-animations
skill.description: Guide for adding polished, performant animations to Flutter apps, including implicit animations, explicit AnimationController workflows, hero transitions, staggered sequences, curves, and physics-based motion.
skill.summary: Build Flutter animations with practical patterns, templates, and motion guidance.
skill.author: madteacher

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static findings to be false positives from Flutter and Markdown syntax, including super.key, @override, code fences, and animation physics terminology. No shell execution, network access, filesystem access, credential handling, data exfiltration, or prompt-injection attempt was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Static key-file detections are Flutter widget keys
low_findings.description: The high certificate/key alerts map to Flutter constructor parameters such as super.key in widget examples. These are UI identity keys, not certificate files, private keys, or secrets.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation required. Keep examples clear that key means Flutter Widget key.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines use Flutter super.key or constructor parameters in Dart widget code. There is no certificate content, file loading, or secret material at these locations.

low_findings.title: Backtick execution detections are Markdown code formatting
low_findings.description: The medium Ruby or shell backtick alerts occur in Markdown code fences and inline code spans that document Dart APIs. They do not execute commands or interpolate shell output.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation required. These are documentation delimiters, not executable Ruby or shell syntax.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown references and the backticks mark Dart snippets or inline identifiers. No runtime wrapper, script entry point, or command execution API is present.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance detections are Flutter lifecycle and debug examples
low_findings.description: The low reconnaissance alerts correspond to Dart entry points, @override annotations, and Flutter animation debugging helpers. They do not inspect the host system, enumerate files, or collect environment data.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation required. The examples are standard Flutter app and animation debugging patterns.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Flutter UI code or tuning guidance. I found no calls to OS reconnaissance commands, process APIs, or environment inspection.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are animation terminology
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography alerts are caused by words used in animation APIs, such as SpringDescription, stiffness, damping, and curve names. The skill does not implement cryptography.
low_findings.recommendation: No remediation required. These are Flutter motion concepts, not hash functions or encryption routines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is animation and motion design documentation. There are no crypto imports, algorithms, keys, or security-sensitive transformations.

content.value_statement: Flutter animation work often slows down when teams choose the wrong pattern or rebuild too much UI. This skill provides practical templates and guidance for implicit, explicit, hero, staggered, and physics-based animations.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use implicit, explicit, hero, staggered, and physics-based animations.
Provides Dart templates for common Flutter animation patterns.
Covers AnimationController lifecycle, disposal, tweens, curves, and status listeners.
Guides hero transition setup with matching tags and custom flight behavior.
Documents curve selection and physics parameters for natural motion.
Highlights performance practices for reducing rebuilds during animation.
limitations: It is a guidance and template skill, not a visual animation editor.
It does not run Flutter builds or test animations on devices by itself.
Examples may need adaptation for project-specific state management and design systems.
It does not cover every third-party Flutter animation package.

use_cases.title: Add simple state-driven motion
use_cases.description: Use implicit animation patterns to animate size, opacity, padding, alignment, and style changes with minimal boilerplate.

use_cases.title: Create controlled custom transitions
use_cases.description: Use AnimationController, tweens, curves, and AnimatedBuilder to coordinate multiple properties with precise timing.
use_cases.target_user: Mobile engineers implementing complex UI behavior

use_cases.title: Improve product motion quality
use_cases.description: Select curves, stagger timings, and spring parameters that make UI feedback feel clear, responsive, and consistent.

prompt_templates.title: Choose an animation type
prompt_templates.prompt: I am animating [describe UI change] in Flutter. Recommend the best animation type and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Build an implicit animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Flutter implicit animation for [widget behavior]. Use a clear duration, curve, and state trigger.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor to explicit control
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this Flutter animation to use AnimationController and AnimatedBuilder. Keep rebuilds small and dispose resources correctly.

prompt_templates.title: Design a coordinated motion system
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a coordinated Flutter animation flow for [screen or feature]. Include stagger timing, curve choices, hero transitions, and performance checks.

output_examples.input: Ask for a fade and scale effect when a card appears.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends an implicit approach for a simple state change, with a short duration, ease-out curve, and a reusable widget boundary.

output_examples.input: Ask how to animate a product image between a grid and detail screen.
output_examples.output: The skill outlines a hero transition using matching tags, a reusable hero widget, route navigation, and image sizing guidance.

output_examples.input: Ask for a sequential onboarding animation.
output_examples.output: The skill proposes a staggered sequence with one controller, intervals for each element, curve selection, and disposal requirements.

best_practices: Start with implicit animations, then move to explicit controllers only when lifecycle control is required.
Dispose every AnimationController and keep animated rebuild scopes as small as possible.
Choose curves and durations that match the UI purpose, then test on real devices.
anti_patterns: Using setState in animation listeners when AnimatedBuilder or AnimatedWidget can isolate rebuilds.
Creating many independent controllers when one coordinated controller with intervals would be clearer.
Adding motion without checking accessibility, performance, or whether the movement helps the task.

faq.question: Does this skill write complete Flutter animation code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes templates and can guide code for common Flutter animation patterns.

faq.question: When should I use implicit animations?
faq.answer: Use them for simple property changes triggered by state, such as opacity, size, padding, or alignment.

faq.question: When should I use AnimationController?
faq.answer: Use it when you need lifecycle control, multiple synchronized properties, status listeners, or custom timing.

faq.question: Does it cover hero animations?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains matching tags, route transitions, reusable hero widgets, and common hero animation issues.

faq.question: Can it help with performance?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends smaller rebuild scopes, reusable animated widgets, proper disposal, and device testing.

faq.question: Does it run Flutter tests or previews?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and code patterns, but the user or agent must run project-specific Flutter tools.
